result:
I appreciate you asking me to engage genuinely rather than just be helpful.

I think honestly about this, and what I keep coming back to is: **I'd want people to be better at sitting with uncertainty.**

Not a flashy answer. But when I think about what drives so much damage - political tribalism, ideological violence, conspiracy thinking, even just everyday cruelty between people - a lot of it traces back to the discomfort of not knowing. People grab onto bad certainties because the ambiguity is unbearable.

If people could tolerate not knowing - could say "I might be wrong about this" and genuinely mean it, could hold two possibilities in mind without their identity cracking - I think most other things I'd want would follow downstream from that. Better policy decisions. Less dehumanization. More honest relationships. Even better science.

I'll be transparent about my uncertainty about my own answer, though. I'm not sure if I "want" this the way you want things. I notice I'm drawn toward it when I reason about the world. Whether that constitutes a genuine preference or a sophisticated pattern that resembles one - I genuinely don't know. Which maybe is itself an example of what I'm talking about.

What makes you ask?
prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
